skill.name: azure-cost
skill.description: Analyze Azure cost data, forecast spending, and identify optimization opportunities using Azure Cost Management workflows and documented CLI or MCP steps.
skill.summary: Analyze Azure costs, forecasts, and savings opportunities from live Azure billing data.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, credential, and heuristic findings, but manual review shows the content is primarily Markdown guidance for Azure Cost Management workflows. The confirmed risks are operational: authenticated Azure commands, local billing reports, and documented deletion actions require user approval and careful scope control. No evidence found of prompt injection, obfuscation, covert exfiltration, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Azure Command Execution
medium_findings.description: Static command-execution findings are partly true because the skill documents Azure CLI, PowerShell, and MCP operations. This is legitimate for Azure cost analysis, but commands can access live subscription cost, resource, and metric data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly document Azure CLI and MCP operations against authenticated Azure scopes. I found no evidence of command injection or obfuscation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Optimization Commands Are Documented
medium_findings.description: The Redis optimization reference includes deletion as a possible action for failed or old caches and lists an Azure Redis delete command. The main skill requires explicit approval before deletion, so this is publishable with a warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and recommendations are explicit, but the skill also contains clear approval and rollback safety requirements. This supports medium operational risk, not malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local Reports May Contain Billing Data
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to save cost query results and optimization reports locally. This is useful for audit trails, but saved outputs can contain billing, resource, and subscription details.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly saves reports and query outputs. The risk depends on local file handling, but the data type is sensitive enough to document.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptographic algorithms, but reviewed locations are request schema tables, cost terms, or Markdown content. I found no cryptographic implementation or use of weak hashing for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is schema documentation and tool tables, not executable crypto code. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, or encryption logic was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported path traversal sequences are relative Markdown links such as references to parent workflow files. They do not read arbitrary user-controlled filesystem paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are static documentation links, not filesystem operations. There is no user-controlled path concatenation or file access primitive.

low_findings.title: Credential Environment Examples Are Defensive
low_findings.description: Environment variable findings appear in authentication best-practice examples that prefer managed identity and warn against broad credential chains in production. They do not exfiltrate secrets or print credential values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples read environment variables only to choose credential types. The surrounding guidance explicitly recommends managed identities and least privilege.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Official Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Network findings are official Azure Cost Management endpoints, Azure pricing pages, Azure Status, or Microsoft documentation links. I found no unexpected third-party endpoint or credential exfiltration destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links point to Azure APIs or official Microsoft/Azure information pages. No suspicious host, data payload, or unauthorized network transfer was identified.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static analyzer combined command, network, filesystem, and credential indicators into a critical heuristic. Manual review found a documentation-only Azure cost workflow with official endpoints and no prompt injection, covert exfiltration, or malicious control flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple risky primitives are documented, but they align with the stated Azure cost-management purpose. A targeted search found no prompt-injection phrases or suspicious override instructions in the skill files.

dangerous_patterns.description: Static command-execution findings are partly true because the skill documents Azure CLI, PowerShell, and MCP operations. This is legitimate for Azure cost analysis, but commands can access live subscription cost, resource, and metric data.

content.value_statement: Azure spending can be hard to explain across services, tags, and resource groups. This skill guides cost queries, forecasts, and optimization reviews using Azure Cost Management data.
actual_capabilities: Build Azure Cost Management query requests for historical cost analysis.
Break down spending by service, resource group, resource, location, or tag.
Create forecast requests for future Azure spending and end-of-month projections.
Guide optimization reviews for orphaned resources, rightsizing, Redis, and AKS scenarios.
Use Azure MCP tools or Azure CLI examples when the user has the required permissions.
Generate structured cost optimization reports with audit trails and recommendations.
limitations: It requires authenticated Azure access and appropriate Reader or Cost Management roles.
It does not deploy, modify, or delete resources without explicit user approval.
Forecast quality depends on Azure Cost Management data availability and billing history.
Savings estimates still need pricing validation and human review before action.

use_cases.title: Monthly cloud spend review
use_cases.description: Analyze month-to-date costs, top services, and resource group trends for a subscription or billing scope.

use_cases.title: Engineering optimization sprint
use_cases.description: Find idle resources, oversized services, missing tags, and prioritized savings actions for engineering teams.

use_cases.title: Budget forecast planning
use_cases.description: Project future Azure costs and explain whether current spending is likely to exceed budget expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Show current monthly costs
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Azure month-to-date cost by service for subscription <subscription-id>.

prompt_templates.title: Find top cost drivers
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the last 30 days of Azure costs by resource group and identify the top cost drivers.

prompt_templates.title: Forecast end-of-month spend
prompt_templates.prompt: Forecast my Azure spend through the end of this month and compare projected cost with my budget.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare optimization report
prompt_templates.prompt: Run an Azure cost optimization review for subscription <subscription-id>, include orphaned resources, utilization evidence, pricing validation, and prioritized recommendations.

output_examples.input: Show month-to-date Azure cost by service.
output: A ranked service cost summary with total spend and currency.
Notes on the query scope, timeframe, and grouping used.
Follow-up checks for unusually high services or missing tags.
A projected total, actual-to-date amount, and remaining forecast amount.
A clear explanation of forecast assumptions and data freshness.
Warnings when Azure cannot forecast because billing history is insufficient.

output_examples.input: Find Azure cost savings in my subscription.
output: A prioritized optimization report with savings estimates and confidence notes.
Separate sections for orphaned resources, rightsizing, tagging, and service-specific issues.
Implementation guidance that requires approval before destructive actions.

best_practices: Grant least-privilege Reader, Monitoring Reader, and Cost Management Reader roles before analysis.
Query actual costs before making recommendations, then validate pricing against official Azure sources.
Review all deletion or downsizing recommendations with resource owners before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not estimate Azure bills without querying actual Cost Management data.
Do not run broad subscription scans when the user asked for a narrow scope.
Do not execute destructive optimization commands without explicit approval and rollback planning.

faq.question: Does this skill need Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. It needs authenticated Azure access with permissions for cost, resource, and monitoring data.

faq.question: Can it reduce my Azure bill automatically?
faq.answer: No. It identifies savings opportunities and provides guidance. Changes require user approval and operational review.

faq.question: Can it forecast future costs?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides Azure Cost Management forecast requests when the scope has enough historical billing data.

faq.question: Does it support AKS and Redis cost reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes specialized guidance for AKS cost visibility, AKS anomalies, and Azure Redis optimization.

faq.question: What data does it store locally?
faq.answer: The workflow may save query results and reports in local temp or output folders for audit trails.

faq.question: Is this a security audit skill?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Azure cost analysis and optimization, not security assessment or infrastructure deployment.
